content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 1, 1999

Maria M. Stewart
City Clerk
City of Santa Monica
1685 Main Street
Post Office Box 2200
Santa Monica, California 90407-2200

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-99-050

Dear Ms. Stewart:

This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance on behalf of
Councilmember Kevin McKeown regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹ Since your request does not pertain to a specific governmental decision, we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Councilmember McKeown from participating in council
discussions and decisions involving funding of the school district?

CONCLUSION

It is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions regarding the school district funding will
have a material financial effect on Councilmember McKeown's economic interests. Therefore,
he may not make, participate in making, or influence the council's governmental decisions with
regard to the school district's funding.

___________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-99-050
Page 2

FACTS

The City of Santa Monica (the "city") participates with and provides funding for joint
projects with the Santa Monica-Malibu Unified School District (the "school district").
Councilmember McKeown is expected to participate in deliberations and vote on motions
involving the approval of funds for the school district.

Councilmember McKeown works as an independent consultant providing computer
services that include network installation and maintenance, equipment and deployment
recommendations, computer repair, software installation, and computer training. One of his
clients is the school district, and it is currently his primary source of income.

As an independent consultant, Councilmember McKeown has a contact person in the
school district to arrange for a provision of services and deals directly with the principal or
teacher at the particular school where the services are needed. Compensation is based on an
hourly basis with funding from the school district's technology budget.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions require that public officials perform their duties
in an impartial manner, free from bias attributable to their personal financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.

Identifying Councilmember McKeown's Economic Interests

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic
interests. The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are enumerated in
Section 87103. There are five kinds of such interests:

•    A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment³ of $1,000 or more, or, in which he or she is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section
87103(a) and (d); Regulation 18703.1(a) and (b).)

___________________________

³ An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent
child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the
official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent
interest or greater. (Section 87103.)

File No. I-99-050
Page 3

•	A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $1,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)

•	A public official has an economic interest in any source of income which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

•	A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $300 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)

•	A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family—this is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

For purposes of this analysis, we assume that Councilmember McKeown has received income of $250 or more from his consulting business in the previous 12 months. (Section 87103(c).) In addition, presumably he has an investment interest in his consulting business of $1,000 or more.⁴ (Section 87103(a).) Finally, as a sole proprietor, he has an economic interest in his business pursuant to Section 87103(d).

In addition, for purposes of the Act, income includes an individual's pro rata share of income received by a business in which he or she has a 10-percent interest or more. (Section 82030.) Since Mr. McKeown owns 100 percent of his business as a sole proprietor, all the business income is attributed to him. Therefore, any clients who have paid $250 or more to his business within a 12 month period are also disqualifying sources of income to him. We assume that the school district has provided his business $250 or more within the last 12 months.

Accordingly, since he has an economic interest in his business and in the school district, he may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use his official position to influence a council decision about school district funding if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on his business or the school district.

The Foreseeability of a Material Financial Effect

Once the official identifies his or her economic interests, the official must evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a governmental decision will have a material financial effect on the economic interests. First, the official must determine whether the economic interests will be directly or indirectly involved in the decision. Based upon the economic interest's degree of involvement, the official must then choose the appropriate materiality

⁴ During our electronic mail communications, Mr. McKeown indicated that he has a computer that he uses for personal and business purposes. In addition, his vehicle is also used for personal and business purposes.

File No. I-99-050
Page 4

standard to use in evaluating whether the financial impact of the decision will be material. After
the official finds the pertinent materiality standard, he or she must decide whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met.

Involvement in Decision

Regulation 18704.1(a) provides guidance on when a business entity or a source of income
is directly involved in a decision. When an economic interest is not directly involved in a
decision, it is indirectly involved for purposes of applying the materiality standards. (Regulation
18704.1(b).) A source of income is directly involved in a decision when that person or business
entity:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made
by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person." (Emphasis added.)

Materiality Standard

The school district is directly involved in any decision pertaining to its funding. If a
source of income is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency, any reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of the decision is deemed to be material. (Regulation 18705.3(a).)

If Mr. McKeown's business entity is not directly involved in the school district funding
decisions, it is indirectly involved for purposes of the materiality thresholds.⁵ When an official
has an economic interest in a business entity indirectly affected by a governmental decision, the
appropriate standard for determining materiality is set forth in Regulation 18705.1(b)(7) (copy
enclosed.) Under this regulation, for small businesses, the effect of a decision is material if:

"(A) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
gross revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or
(B) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(C) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more."

⁵ Presumably, any funding decisions do not affect any contract with Mr. McKeown's business.

File No. I-99-050
Page 5

Once the appropriate materiality standard is ascertained, it must be determined whether it
is reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706.)
When an effect of a decision is deemed to be material, such as here, with regard to the school
district, the official must determine whether it is substantially likely that any financial effect will
occur as a result of the decision. Since a decision to provide funding to a school district will
have some financial effect on the school district, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on the school district.

With regard to the business entity, the issue is whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the materiality standard in Regulation 18705.1 will be met as a result of the decision. Since you
have not provided any facts regarding the funding process, we are unable to determine whether it
is reasonably foreseeable that this materiality standard will be met. For example, we do not
know whether the city council has any control over the amount of funds the school district may
spend on the type of services Mr. McKeown provides.

If Mr. McKeown is disqualified as to either his source of income, the school district, or
his business, he cannot participate in any city decisions.

Public Generally

Under the public generally exception, even if a public official has what would otherwise
be a conflict of interest, he or she is not disqualified if the financial effect on him or her is
indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation 18707 et
seq.) For the public generally exception to apply, a decision must affect the official's interests in
substantially the same manner as it would affect a significant segment of the public. There are no
factors to indicate that the public generally exception applies in this case.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By: Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant, Legal Division

QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Councilmember McKeown from participating in council
discussions and decisions involving funding of the school district?

CONCLUSION: It is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions regarding the school district funding will
have a material financial effect on Councilmember McKeown’s economic interests. Therefore,
he may not make, participate in making, or influence the council’s governmental decisions with
regard to the school district’s funding.2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
